m_Item1.QuestionText:
How can I dynamically allocate a two-dimensional array?
   1) int **array1 = (int **)malloc(nrows * sizeof(int *));
   for(i = 0; i < nrows; i++)
   array1[i] = (int *)malloc(ncolumns * sizeof(int));

   2) int **array2 = (int **)malloc(nrows * sizeof(int *));
   array2[0] = (int *)malloc(nrows * ncolumns * sizeof(int));
   for(i = 1; i < nrows; i++)
   array2[i] = array2[0] + i * ncolumns;

   3) int *array3 = (int *)malloc(nrows * ncolumns * sizeof(int));
m_Item1.Explanation:
In first 2 ways memory is getting allocated firstly for rows & then for every columns in rows.
In 3rd way memory is getting allocated for (row*col) these many blocks . so all are correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following sorting algorithm is not stable
m_Item1.Explanation:
Consider array 21 20a 20b 12 11 8 7 (already in max-heap format)

here 20a = 20b just to differentiate the order we represent them as 20a and 20b

While heapsort first 21 is removed and placed in the last index then 20a is removed and placed in last but one index and 20b in the last but two index so after heap sort the array looks like

7 8 11 12 20b 20a 21.

It does not preserve the order of elements and hence can't be stable

Much more simpler to analyze than that of worst case

m_Item1.QuestionText: The following sequence of operation is performed on stack : push(1),push(2),pop,push(3),push(4),pop,pop,pop,push(5),pop. What is the sequence of popped out values?
m_Item1.Explanation:
before 1st pop elements in stack are (1, 2), so 2 will poped out first then elements are (1, 3, 4), so poped elements will be 4, 3, 1 & at the end 5 is present in stack so 5 will be popped out at end.
So 2, 4, 3, 1, 5
